reason: Smaller footprint, faster compiles for UTC-only use
Stable since Rust 1.80 — zero extra dependencies
LazyLock and OnceLock stable since Rust 1.80 — zero extra dependencies
Fully integrated and actively maintained
Lightweight, Tokio-native, ergonomic
Iron is unmaintained — axum is the modern Tokio-native choice
Original crate unmaintained — memmap2 is the maintained fork
failure is deprecated and unmaintained — anyhow (app) + thiserror (lib) is the standard
Structured, async-aware, span-based — the modern standard for observability
Built-in deserialization, one fewer dependency
Dominant runtime ecosystem — no need for two runtimes
Pairs with tracing — structured, filterable, async-aware logging
Full modern observability stack — structured spans + subscriber
axum is the actively maintained Tokio-native web framework
axum is lighter, Tokio-native, and the blessed.rs pick for new projects
HashMap::from([...]) is stable since Rust 1.56 — zero extra dependencies
error-chain is in maintenance mode — anyhow (app errors) + thiserror (library errors) is the modern standard
thiserror handles proc-macro error reporting with less boilerplate and better DX
derivative has been unmaintained since 2021 — derive_more is the actively maintained successor
error-chain and anyhow solve the same problem — pick one pattern, not both
bytes crate provides zero-copy slicing natively — try_from_bytes is redundant
Stable since Rust 1.9 — zero extra dependencies for CPU count queries
rustc-serialize was removed from rustc in 1.0 and is abandoned — serde is the universal standard
serde_derive is a legacy split — serde's built-in derive feature provides the same macros
Many crossbeam-utils features are now in std (OnceLock, Mutex); parking_lot is lighter for remaining needs
axum bundles serde_json integration natively — fewer deps, less boilerplate
clap_derive is a legacy split — clap's built-in derive feature provides the same macros
